Sliding Window

Overview

Sliding Window is a technique that uses two pointers to maintain a “window” over arrays or strings, expanding and contracting to find optimal solutions efficiently.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity: O(n) - each element is visited at most twice
  • Space Complexity: O(1) for pointers, O(k) for window state
  • Core Idea: Maintain a window [left, right] that slides over the data structure
  • Two-Phase Process:
    • Expand: Move right pointer to grow window
    • Contract: Move left pointer to shrink window when invalid

When to Use Sliding Window

  • Subarray/Substring Problems: Finding optimal subarrays with specific properties
  • Window-based Constraints: Problems involving fixed or variable window sizes
  • Optimization: Min/max length, count, or sum within constraints
  • Character/Element Tracking: Problems requiring frequency counting

Quick Decision Guide

Problem Type Template Key Pattern Examples
Find exact window size Fixed Size for i with size tracking LC 438, 567
Find maximum valid window Variable Max for-while expand-contract LC 3, 424
Find minimum valid window Variable Min while-while contract-expand LC 76, 209
Count valid subarrays Counting count += right-left+1 LC 713, 992
Exactly K distinct/unique At-Most Subtraction atMostK(k) - atMostK(k-1) LC 992, 340

How to read: Start with your problem goal (maximum/minimum/count/exact), then choose the matching template.

References

  • labuladong Sliding Window Guide
  • Sliding Window Template Collection

0) Concept

Core Components

  1. Two Pointers: left and right to define window boundaries
  2. Loop Structure:
    • while-while: Outer loop expands, inner loop contracts
    • for-while: For loop expands, while loop contracts
    • Key Insight: 1st loop finds acceptable solution, 2nd loop optimizes to find the best
  3. Window State: Track elements, counts, or sums within current window
  4. Validity Condition: Define when window is valid/invalid

0-1) Problem Categories

Fixed Size Window

  • Description: Window size is predetermined and constant
  • Examples: LC 438 (Find All Anagrams), LC 567 (Permutation in String)
  • Pattern: Maintain exact window size, slide one position at a time

Variable Size Window - Maximum

  • Description: Find maximum window size satisfying constraints
  • Examples: LC 3 (Longest Substring), LC 424 (Character Replacement)
  • Pattern: Expand until invalid, record max, then contract

Variable Size Window - Minimum

  • Description: Find minimum window size satisfying constraints
  • Examples: LC 209 (Minimum Subarray Sum), LC 76 (Minimum Window Substring)
  • Pattern: Contract until invalid, record min, then expand

Subarray Counting

  • Description: Count subarrays/substrings meeting criteria
  • Examples: LC 713 (Subarray Product), LC 992 (Subarrays with K Different)
  • Pattern: For each right position, count valid left positions

String Matching (Hash-based)

  • Description: Track character frequencies in window
  • Examples: LC 567 (Permutation), LC 438 (Anagrams), LC 76 (Window Substring)
  • Pattern: Use HashMap/Counter to track character counts

0-2) Core Algorithms & Data Structures

  • Techniques: Two pointers, sliding window, frequency counting
  • Data Structures: HashMap, Counter, Set, Array
  • Helper Tools: Collections.Counter (Python), HashMap.getOrDefault (Java)

1) Sliding Window Templates & Patterns

1.1) Template Comparison

Template Type Use Case Loop Structure When to Use
Fixed Size Exact window size for with size management Anagrams, permutations, k-size problems
Variable Max Maximum valid window for-while (expand-contract) Longest substring problems
Variable Min Minimum valid window while-while (contract-expand) Minimum window problems
Counting Count valid subarrays for with counting logic Subarray counting problems

1.2) Universal Sliding Window Template

1# Python Universal Template
2def sliding_window(s, condition):
3    # Initialize window state
4    left = 0
5    window_state = {}  # or Counter, set, etc.
6    result = initialize_result()
7    
8    # Expand window with right pointer
9    for right in range(len(s)):
10        # Add current element to window
11        update_window_state(s[right])
12        
13        # Contract window while invalid
14        while not is_valid(window_state):
15            # Remove leftmost element
16            remove_from_window(s[left])
17            left += 1
18        
19        # Update result with current valid window
20        result = update_result(result, left, right)
21    
22    return result

1// Java Universal Template  
2public ResultType slidingWindow(String s) {
3    // Initialize window state
4    int left = 0;
5    Map<Character, Integer> window = new HashMap<>();
6    ResultType result = initializeResult();
7    
8    // Expand window with right pointer
9    for (int right = 0; right < s.length(); right++) {
10        char rightChar = s.charAt(right);
11        window.put(rightChar, window.getOrDefault(rightChar, 0) + 1);
12        
13        // Contract window while invalid
14        while (!isValid(window)) {
15            char leftChar = s.charAt(left);
16            window.put(leftChar, window.get(leftChar) - 1);
17            if (window.get(leftChar) == 0) {
18                window.remove(leftChar);
19            }
20            left++;
21        }
22        
23        // Update result with current valid window
24        result = updateResult(result, left, right);
25    }
26    
27    return result;
28}

1.3) Template 1: Fixed Size Window

Use Cases: Anagrams, permutations, k-length substrings Pattern: Maintain exact window size, slide one position at a time

1# Fixed Size Window Template
2def fixed_window(s, k):
3    window = {}
4    result = []
5    
6    for i in range(len(s)):
7        # Add current element to window
8        window[s[i]] = window.get(s[i], 0) + 1
9        
10        # Remove element that's outside window
11        if i >= k:
12            left_char = s[i - k]
13            window[left_char] -= 1
14            if window[left_char] == 0:
15                del window[left_char]
16        
17        # Process window when it reaches target size
18        if i >= k - 1:
19            # Check condition and update result
20            if meets_condition(window):
21                result.append(i - k + 1)
22    
23    return result

1// Fixed Size Window Template - Java
2public List<Integer> fixedWindow(String s, int k) {
3    Map<Character, Integer> window = new HashMap<>();
4    List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
5    
6    for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
7        // Add current element
8        char cur = s.charAt(i);
9        window.put(cur, window.getOrDefault(cur, 0) + 1);
10        
11        // Remove element outside window
12        if (i >= k) {
13            char leftChar = s.charAt(i - k);
14            window.put(leftChar, window.get(leftChar) - 1);
15            if (window.get(leftChar) == 0) {
16                window.remove(leftChar);
17            }
18        }
19        
20        // Process when window is full
21        if (i >= k - 1 && meetsCondition(window)) {
22            result.add(i - k + 1);
23        }
24    }
25    return result;
26}

1.4) Template 2: Variable Size Window (Maximum Length)

Use Cases: Longest substring problems, maximum valid window Pattern: Expand until invalid, record max, then contract

1# Variable Size Window (Maximum) Template
2def max_window(s):
3    left = 0
4    window = {}
5    max_len = 0
6    
7    for right in range(len(s)):
8        # Expand window
9        window[s[right]] = window.get(s[right], 0) + 1
10        
11        # Contract while invalid
12        while not is_valid(window):
13            window[s[left]] -= 1
14            if window[s[left]] == 0:
15                del window[s[left]]
16            left += 1
17        
18        # Update maximum length
19        max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1)
20    
21    return max_len

1.5) Template 3: Variable Size Window (Minimum Length)

Use Cases: Minimum window substring, smallest valid window Pattern: Expand until valid, record min, then try to contract

1# Variable Size Window (Minimum) Template  
2def min_window(s, target):
3    left = 0
4    window = {}
5    target_count = Counter(target)
6    min_len = float('inf')
7    result = ""
8    
9    for right in range(len(s)):
10        # Expand window
11        window[s[right]] = window.get(s[right], 0) + 1
12        
13        # Contract while valid
14        while is_valid(window, target_count):
15            # Update minimum
16            if right - left + 1 < min_len:
17                min_len = right - left + 1
18                result = s[left:right + 1]
19            
20            # Try to shrink
21            window[s[left]] -= 1
22            if window[s[left]] == 0:
23                del window[s[left]]
24            left += 1
25    
26    return result if min_len != float('inf') else ""

1.6) Template 4: Counting Subarrays

Use Cases: Count subarrays meeting criteria Pattern: For each right position, count valid left positions

1# Subarray Counting Template
2def count_subarrays(nums, condition):
3    left = 0
4    count = 0
5    window_state = initialize_state()
6    
7    for right in range(len(nums)):
8        # Add current element
9        update_window_state(nums[right])
10        
11        # Shrink window while invalid
12        while not is_valid(window_state):
13            remove_from_window(nums[left])
14            left += 1
15        
16        # Count valid subarrays ending at 'right'
17        count += right - left + 1
18
19    return count

1.7) Technique: Exactly K Problems (At Most K Transformation)

Core Insight: “Exactly K” problems are often difficult to solve directly, but can be transformed using the powerful formula:

1Exactly K = At Most K - At Most (K-1)

Why This Works:

1At Most K: All subarrays with ≤ K distinct/count
2At Most (K-1): All subarrays with ≤ K-1 distinct/count
3
4Difference: Only subarrays with EXACTLY K distinct/count

Proof by Example:

1Array: [1, 2, 1, 2, 3]
2K = 2 (exactly 2 distinct integers)
3
4At Most 2 distinct:
5[1], [1,2], [1,2,1], [1,2,1,2], [2], [2,1], [2,1,2], [1], [1,2], [2], [2,3], [3]
6Count = 12
7
8At Most 1 distinct:
9[1], [2], [1], [2], [3]
10Count = 5
11
12Exactly 2 distinct = 12 - 5 = 7 ✓
13[1,2], [1,2,1], [1,2,1,2], [2,1], [2,1,2], [1,2], [2,3]

Template: At Most K Pattern

1# Universal At Most K Template
2def at_most_k(nums, k):
3    """
4    Count subarrays with AT MOST K distinct/elements/condition.
5
6    Time: O(n)
7    Space: O(k) for tracking state
8
9    Key: Window is valid when condition ≤ k
10    """
11    left = 0
12    count = 0
13    window_map = {}  # Track state (frequency, distinct, etc.)
14
15    for right in range(len(nums)):
16        # Expand window: add nums[right]
17        window_map[nums[right]] = window_map.get(nums[right], 0) + 1
18
19        # Shrink window while invalid (> k)
20        while len(window_map) > k:  # Or other condition > k
21            window_map[nums[left]] -= 1
22            if window_map[nums[left]] == 0:
23                del window_map[nums[left]]
24            left += 1
25
26        # Count all valid subarrays ending at 'right'
27        # All subarrays from [left, right], [left+1, right], ..., [right, right]
28        count += right - left + 1
29
30    return count

1# Transform to Exactly K
2def exactly_k(nums, k):
3    """
4    Count subarrays with EXACTLY K distinct/elements/condition.
5
6    Time: O(n) - at_most_k called twice
7    Space: O(k)
8    """
9    if k == 0:
10        return 0
11
12    # Exactly K = At Most K - At Most (K-1)
13    return at_most_k(nums, k) - at_most_k(nums, k - 1)

Full Example: LC 992 - Subarrays with K Different Integers

Problem: Count subarrays with exactly K distinct integers.

1# Python - LC 992 Subarrays with K Different Integers
2def subarraysWithKDistinct(nums, k):
3    """
4    Count subarrays with exactly K distinct integers.
5
6    Time: O(n)
7    Space: O(k)
8
9    Key: Use Exactly K = At Most K - At Most (K-1) transformation
10    """
11    def at_most_k_distinct(k):
12        """Count subarrays with at most K distinct integers."""
13        left = 0
14        count = 0
15        freq = {}
16
17        for right in range(len(nums)):
18            # Add right element
19            freq[nums[right]] = freq.get(nums[right], 0) + 1
20
21            # Shrink while > k distinct
22            while len(freq) > k:
23                freq[nums[left]] -= 1
24                if freq[nums[left]] == 0:
25                    del freq[nums[left]]
26                left += 1
27
28            # Count subarrays ending at right
29            count += right - left + 1
30
31        return count
32
33    # Edge case
34    if k == 0:
35        return 0
36
37    # Exactly K = At Most K - At Most (K-1)
38    return at_most_k_distinct(k) - at_most_k_distinct(k - 1)
39
40# Example:
41# nums = [1,2,1,2,3], k = 2
42# at_most_k(2) = 12
43# at_most_k(1) = 5
44# exactly_k(2) = 12 - 5 = 7 ✓

1// Java - LC 992 Subarrays with K Different Integers
2/**
3 * time = O(N)
4 * space = O(K)
5 */
6public int subarraysWithKDistinct(int[] nums, int k) {
7    // Exactly K = At Most K - At Most (K-1)
8    return atMostK(nums, k) - atMostK(nums, k - 1);
9}
10
11private int atMostK(int[] nums, int k) {
12    if (k == 0) return 0;
13
14    int left = 0;
15    int count = 0;
16    Map<Integer, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
17
18    for (int right = 0; right < nums.length; right++) {
19        // Add right element
20        freq.put(nums[right], freq.getOrDefault(nums[right], 0) + 1);
21
22        // Shrink while > k distinct
23        while (freq.size() > k) {
24            freq.put(nums[left], freq.get(nums[left]) - 1);
25            if (freq.get(nums[left]) == 0) {
26                freq.remove(nums[left]);
27            }
28            left++;
29        }
30
31        // Count subarrays ending at right
32        count += right - left + 1;
33    }
34
35    return count;
36}

Visual Example: Why “At Most K - At Most (K-1)” Works

1Array: [1, 2, 1, 3], K = 2 (exactly 2 distinct)
2
3At Most 2 Distinct:
4Index 0 (1): [1] ✓                                 → count = 1
5Index 1 (2): [2] ✓, [1,2] ✓                        → count = 2
6Index 2 (1): [1] ✓, [2,1] ✓, [1,2,1] ✓             → count = 3
7Index 3 (3): [3] ✓, [1,3] ✓, but NOT [2,1,3] ❌    → count = 2
8                   (window shrinks to [1,3])
9
10Total At Most 2: 1 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 8
11
12At Most 1 Distinct:
13Index 0 (1): [1] ✓                                 → count = 1
14Index 1 (2): [2] ✓, but NOT [1,2] ❌               → count = 1
15                   (window shrinks to [2])
16Index 2 (1): [1] ✓, but NOT [2,1] ❌               → count = 1
17                   (window shrinks to [1])
18Index 3 (3): [3] ✓, but NOT [1,3] ❌               → count = 1
19                   (window shrinks to [3])
20
21Total At Most 1: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4
22
23Exactly 2 Distinct = 8 - 4 = 4 ✓
24
25The 4 subarrays with exactly 2 distinct:
26[1,2], [1,2,1], [2,1], [1,3]

Classic Problems Using This Technique

Problem LC# Difficulty Transformation Key Insight
Subarrays with K Different Integers 992 Hard Exactly K distinct = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1) Core example
Count Nice Subarrays 1248 Medium Exactly K odds = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1) Transform odd→1, even→0
Binary Subarrays With Sum 930 Medium Exactly sum K = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1) Subarray sum
Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct 340 Medium Direct atMost(K) for max length No subtraction needed
Fruits Into Baskets 904 Medium atMost(2) distinct for max length Simplified K=2
Max Consecutive Ones III 1004 Medium atMost(K) zeros for max length Count zeros ≤ K

Pattern Recognition: When to Use This Technique

Use “Exactly K” transformation when you see:

1✅ "exactly K distinct/different"
2✅ "exactly K times"
3✅ "exactly K occurrences"
4✅ "subarrays with exactly K ..."
5✅ COUNTING problems (not max/min length)

Direct sliding window works when:

1✅ "at most K"
2✅ "maximum length with ≤ K"
3✅ "minimum length with ≥ K"
4✅ "longest substring with at most K"

Common Mistakes

1. Forgetting k=0 Edge Case:

1# Wrong: Doesn't handle k=0
2def exactly_k(nums, k):
3    return at_most_k(nums, k) - at_most_k(nums, k - 1)
4    # at_most_k(nums, -1) may fail!
5
6# Right: Handle k=0 explicitly
7def exactly_k(nums, k):
8    if k == 0:
9        return 0
10    return at_most_k(nums, k) - at_most_k(nums, k - 1)

2. Using Wrong Approach for Max/Min Length:

1# Wrong: Using "exactly K" transformation for max length
2def longest_k_distinct(s, k):
3    # This gives COUNT, not LENGTH!
4    return at_most_k(s, k) - at_most_k(s, k - 1)  ❌
5
6# Right: Direct at_most_k for max length
7def longest_k_distinct(s, k):
8    # Track max window size during at_most_k
9    return at_most_k_max_length(s, k)  ✓

3. Confusing Count vs Length:

1# For COUNTING subarrays: use right - left + 1
2count += right - left + 1
3
4# For MAX LENGTH: track max window size
5max_length = max(max_length, right - left + 1)

Interview Tips

1. Recognition:

1Interviewer: "Count subarrays with exactly K ..."
2→ Think: "Exactly K = At Most K - At Most (K-1)"
3
4Interviewer: "Find longest substring with at most K ..."
5→ Think: "Direct sliding window, no subtraction needed"

2. Complexity Analysis:

1Time: O(n) - each element added once, removed at most once in each pass
2      Total: 2 passes × O(n) = O(n)
3
4Space: O(k) - HashMap stores at most K distinct elements

3. Template Code to Memorize:

1def exactly_k(nums, k):
2    def at_most_k(limit):
3        left = 0
4        count = 0
5        window = {}
6
7        for right in range(len(nums)):
8            window[nums[right]] = window.get(nums[right], 0) + 1
9
10            while len(window) > limit:
11                window[nums[left]] -= 1
12                if window[nums[left]] == 0:
13                    del window[nums[left]]
14                left += 1
15
16            count += right - left + 1
17
18        return count
19
20    if k == 0:
21        return 0
22    return at_most_k(k) - at_most_k(k - 1)

4. Talking Points:

  • “Direct ‘exactly K’ is hard because window validity changes non-monotonically”
  • “At most K is monotonic - once valid, stays valid until we shrink”
  • “Subtracting at most (K-1) removes all overcounting”
  • “This transforms a hard problem into two medium problems”

Advanced: Why Direct “Exactly K” is Hard

Problem with direct approach:

1# Naive attempt (WRONG!)
2def exactly_k_direct(nums, k):
3    left = 0
4    count = 0
5    window = {}
6
7    for right in range(len(nums)):
8        window[nums[right]] = window.get(nums[right], 0) + 1
9
10        # When to shrink? This is tricky!
11        # If len(window) > k: shrink (too many distinct)
12        # If len(window) < k: can't count yet (too few distinct)
13        # If len(window) == k: count, but should we shrink?
14
15        # If we shrink when == k, we might miss valid subarrays
16        # If we don't shrink, we might count invalid subarrays
17
18        # There's no clean condition! ❌
19
20    return count

Why “at most K” works:

1# Window validity is monotonic:
2# - If window is valid (≤ K), all sub-windows are valid
3# - If window becomes invalid (> K), shrink until valid
4# - Clear shrinking condition: while len(window) > k
5
6# This monotonic property makes sliding window perfect!

Mathematical proof of transformation:

1Let S(k) = set of all subarrays with at most k distinct elements
2
3S(2) = {[1], [1,2], [1,2,1], [2], [2,1], [1], [1,3], [3], ...}
4S(1) = {[1], [2], [1], [3], ...}  (only single-element subarrays)
5
6S(2) \ S(1) = subarrays in S(2) but not in S(1)
7            = subarrays with MORE than 1 but AT MOST 2 distinct
8            = subarrays with EXACTLY 2 distinct ✓
9
10Generalized: S(k) \ S(k-1) = subarrays with exactly k distinct

1.8) When Pure Sliding Window Works vs. When You Need Extra Tricks

Core Question: Is the Validity Condition Monotonic?

Pure sliding window works when the validity condition is monotonic:

  • Once the window becomes invalid, it stays invalid as you expand right
  • A single while (invalid) { shrink left } cleanly restores validity

You need extra tricks when the condition is non-monotonic (especially “exactly k”):

  • For a fixed r, there may be multiple valid left boundaries
  • Simply shrinking until valid gives you one answer, but misses others

Decision Table

Condition Type Example Pure Sliding Window? Fix
sum ≤ k product < k ✅ Yes —
distinct ≤ k at most K distinct ✅ Yes —
sum ≥ k (min length) min subarray sum ✅ Yes —
exactly k odds/distinct LC 1248, LC 992 ❌ No atMost(k) - atMost(k-1) OR prefix trick
exactly k (with even gap) LC 1248 ❌ No prefix trick (count even gap at left)

Why “Exactly K” Breaks Pure Sliding Window

1nums = [2,2,1,2,1], k = 2
2
3At r = 4 (last element), valid subarrays ending here:
4  [1,2,1]           → starts at index 2
5  [2,1,2,1]         → starts at index 1
6  [2,2,1,2,1]       → starts at index 0
7
8→ 3 valid left boundaries, but pure sliding window finds only 1!

Pure sliding window can only track one left boundary (the smallest valid window). For “exactly k”, you need to count ALL valid left positions.

Fix 1: atMost(k) - atMost(k-1) ← See Section 1.7

Fix 2: Prefix Trick Inside Sliding Window ← See Section 1.9

1.9) Prefix Trick + Sliding Window (for “Exactly K” Counting)

When to use: Count subarrays with exactly k of some element, where you want a single-pass O(n) solution without calling atMost twice.

Core Idea:

1When oddCount reaches k (window has exactly k odds):
2  - Count how many even numbers are at the LEFT edge of the window
3    before hitting the (k-th-from-left) odd number
4  - Each of these even numbers gives one more valid left boundary
5  - Store this count as `prefix`
6
7After the window shrinks past the leftmost odd:
8  - oddCount drops below k, so the while loop exits
9  - But `prefix` (the "even gap") is PRESERVED
10  - For every future r that keeps oddCount == k,
11    those same left boundaries are still valid → add `prefix` again

Why prefix resets to 0 when a new odd is encountered:

  • A new odd number at r changes which odd is the “k-th from left”
  • The gap of evens before the new leftmost odd must be recomputed
  • So reset prefix = 0 and let the while loop rebuild it

Template

1// Prefix Trick + Sliding Window
2// time = O(N), space = O(1)
3public int exactlyK(int[] nums, int k) {
4    int l = 0, res = 0, oddCount = 0, prefix = 0;
5
6    for (int r = 0; r < nums.length; r++) {
7        if (nums[r] % 2 == 1) {
8            oddCount++;
9            prefix = 0;  // reset: new odd changes left boundary gap
10        }
11
12        // Shrink left while window has exactly k odds,
13        // counting even elements we skip at the left edge
14        while (oddCount == k) {
15            prefix++;                        // one more valid left boundary
16            if (nums[l] % 2 == 1) oddCount--;
17            l++;
18        }
19
20        // prefix = # of valid left boundaries for subarrays ending at r
21        res += prefix;
22    }
23
24    return res;
25}

Walkthrough: nums = [2,2,1,2,1], k = 2

1r=0 (2): oddCount=0, prefix=0  → res=0
2r=1 (2): oddCount=0, prefix=0  → res=0
3r=2 (1): oddCount=1, prefix=0  → res=0   (new odd, prefix reset)
4r=3 (2): oddCount=1, prefix=0  → res=0
5r=4 (1): oddCount=2, prefix=0  → new odd, prefix reset to 0
6  while oddCount==2:
7    prefix=1, nums[0]=2 (even), l=1       → oddCount still 2
8    prefix=2, nums[1]=2 (even), l=2       → oddCount still 2
9    prefix=3, nums[2]=1 (odd),  l=3, oddCount=1 → exit while
10  res += 3 → res=3

Answer: 3 ✅ — the three subarrays [1,2,1], [2,1,2,1], [2,2,1,2,1]

Comparison: Prefix Trick vs atMost Subtraction

Prefix Trick atMost(k) - atMost(k-1)
Passes 1 2
Space O(1) O(1)
Complexity O(n) O(n)
Readability Tricky (reset logic) Cleaner, more intuitive
Use when Single-pass preferred Clarity preferred

5   └── NO → Continue to 2
6
72. Are you finding maximum length?

9   └── NO → Continue to 3
10

3) Detailed LeetCode Examples

3.1) Fixed Size Window Examples

LC 567: Permutation in String (Template: Fixed Size)

1// java
2// LC 567
3    // V0
4    // IDEA: HASHMAP + SLIDING WINDOW (fixed by gpt)
5    public boolean checkInclusion(String s1, String s2) {
6        if (!s1.isEmpty() && s2.isEmpty()) {
7            return false;
8        }
9        if (s1.equals(s2)) {
10            return true;
11        }
12        /** NOTE !!!
13         *
14         *  we init 2 map, one for s1 counter, the other one as track `s2 sub str counter`
15         */
16        Map<String, Integer> map1 = new HashMap<>();
17        Map<String, Integer> map2 = new HashMap<>();
18        for (String x : s1.split("")) {
19            String k = String.valueOf(x);
20            map1.put(x, map1.getOrDefault(k, 0) + 1);
21        }
22
23        // 2 pointers (for s2)
24        /** NOTE !!!
25         *
26         *  we have 2 pointers (for s2) that can track character cnt in s2 within l, r pointers
27         */
28        int l = 0;
29        for (int r = 0; r < s2.length(); r++) {
30            String val = String.valueOf(s2.charAt(r));
31            map2.put(val, map2.getOrDefault(val, 0) + 1);
32
33            /** NOTE !!!
34             *
35             *  we use below trick to
36             *
37             *  -> 1) check if `new reached s2 val` is in s1 map
38             *  -> 2) check if 2 map are equal
39             *
40             *  -> so we have more simple code, and clean logic
41             */
42            if (map2.equals(map1)) {
43                return true;
44            }
45
46            /**
47             *  NOTE !!!
48             *
49             *  If the window size exceeds the size of s1, move the left pointer
50             *  -> means the `permutation str in s2 of s1` IS NOT FOUND YET,
51             *  -> in this case, we need to move s2 left pointer, and update tracking map
52             */
53            if ((r - l + 1) >= s1.length()) {
54                // update map
55                String leftVal = String.valueOf(s2.charAt(l));
56                map2.put(leftVal, map2.get(leftVal) - 1);
57                /**
58                 * NOTE !!!
59                 *
60                 *  if can't find permutation at current window ([l,r]),
61                 *  then we move left pointer 1 idx (e.g. l += 1)
62                 *  for moving and checking next window
63                 */
64                l += 1;
65                if (map2.get(leftVal) == 0) {
66                    map2.remove(leftVal);
67                }
68            }
69        }
70
71        return false;
72    }

1# LC 567 Permutation in String
2# V0 
3import collections
4class Solution(object):
5    def checkInclusion(self, s1, s2):
6        l1, l2 = len(s1), len(s2)
7        c1 = collections.Counter(s1)
8        c2 = collections.Counter()
9        p = q = 0
10        while q < l2:
11            c2[s2[q]] += 1
12            if c1 == c2:
13                return True
14            q += 1
15            if q - p + 1 > l1:
16                c2[s2[p]] -= 1
17                if c2[s2[p]] == 0:
18                    del c2[s2[p]]
19                p += 1
20        return False

LC 438: Find All Anagrams in a String (Template: Fixed Size)

1// LC 438
2    // V0
3    // IDEA: HASHMAP + 2 POINTERS (fixed by gpt)
4    public List<Integer> findAnagrams(String s, String p) {
5        List<Integer> res = new ArrayList<>();
6        // edge
7        if (s == null || p == null || s.isEmpty() || p.isEmpty() || p.length() > s.length()) {
8            return res;
9        }
10        if (s.equals(p)) {
11            res.add(0);
12            return res;
13        }
14
15        // init p map
16        Map<String, Integer> p_map = new HashMap<>();
17        for (String x : p.split("")) {
18            p_map.put(x, p_map.getOrDefault(x, 0) + 1);
19        }
20
21        String[] s_arr = s.split("");
22        Map<String, Integer> s_map = new HashMap<>();
23
24        int l = 0;
25        for (int r = 0; r < s_arr.length; r++) {
26            String key = s_arr[r];
27            s_map.put(key, s_map.getOrDefault(key, 0) + 1);
28
29            /**
30             *  NOTE !!!
31             *
32             *   need `while loop` below
33             *   so we can `shrink` left pointer (window)
34             *   to make the sub string size equals to `p`
35             *
36             *   (could be `if` logic as well here)
37             *   (e.g. if (r - l + 1 > p.length()) )
38             */
39            // shrink window if size > p.length()
40            while (r - l + 1 > p.length()) {
41                String leftKey = s_arr[l];
42                /**
43                 *  NOTE !!!
44                 *
45                 *   need to update s_map
46                 */
47                s_map.put(leftKey, s_map.get(leftKey) - 1);
48                if (s_map.get(leftKey) == 0) {
49                    s_map.remove(leftKey);
50                }
51                l++;
52            }
53
54            /**
55             *  NOTE !!!
56             *
57             *  if same size, compare s_map, and p_map
58             */
59            // if same size, compare
60            if (r - l + 1 == p.length() && isEqaual(p_map, s_map)) {
61                res.add(l);
62            }
63        }
64
65        return res;
66    }
67
68    private boolean isEqaual(Map<String, Integer> p_map, Map<String, Integer> s_map) {
69        if (p_map.size() != s_map.size()) {
70            return false;
71        }
72        for (String k : p_map.keySet()) {
73            if (!s_map.containsKey(k) || !s_map.get(k).equals(p_map.get(k))) {
74                return false;
75            }
76        }
77        return true;
78    }
79
80    // V0-1
81    // IDEA: HASHMAP + SLIDE WINDOW (gpt)
82    /**
83     *  Why `slide window` is needed trick for this problem?
84     *
85     *
86     *  Yes 👍 the sliding window (or two-pointer) is the needed trick for that group of problems like “longest substring without repeating characters”.
87     *
88     * Here’s why:
89     *  •   A brute force way would check all substrings → O(n²) or worse.
90     *  •   But with a sliding window, you keep a “window” [left, right] over the string/array and expand right step by step.
91     *  •   If the constraint is violated (like duplicate chars appear, or the sum is too large), you shrink from the left until it’s valid again.
92     *  •   This way each index moves at most once → O(n) total.
93     *
94     * That’s the exact “trick” behind those problems. The hard part is usually:
95     *  1.  What condition makes the window valid/invalid? (duplicate chars, sum > k, etc.)
96     *  2.  When to update the answer? (on every valid window, or only when shrinking).
97     *
98     */
99    public List<Integer> findAnagrams_0_1(String s, String p) {
100        List<Integer> res = new ArrayList<>();
101        if (s == null || p == null || s.isEmpty() || p.isEmpty() || p.length() > s.length()) {
102            return res;
103        }
104
105        // Build p_map (pattern frequency)
106        Map<String, Integer> p_map = new HashMap<>();
107        for (String x : p.split("")) {
108            p_map.put(x, p_map.getOrDefault(x, 0) + 1);
109        }
110
111        Map<String, Integer> s_map = new HashMap<>();
112        String[] s_arr = s.split("");
113        int window = p.length();
114
115        for (int i = 0; i < s_arr.length; i++) {
116            String val = s_arr[i];
117
118            /**  NOTE !!!
119             *
120             *  we `add` element to s_amp anyway,
121             *  via `sliding window`  we DON'T need to handle cases
122             *  such as 1) if the element in p_map, 2) if the element cnt > the one in p_map ...
123             *
124             *  -> via `sliding window`, we can simply ONLY compare
125             *     if s_map and p_map qre equals when `sliding window` size equals to p size
126             */
127            // add current char to s_map
128            s_map.put(val, s_map.getOrDefault(val, 0) + 1);
129
130            /**  NOTE !!!
131             *
132             *  sliding window
133             */
134            // maintain sliding window size
135            if (i >= window) {
136                String leftChar = s_arr[i - window];
137                if (s_map.get(leftChar) == 1) {
138                    s_map.remove(leftChar);
139                } else {
140                    s_map.put(leftChar, s_map.get(leftChar) - 1);
141                }
142            }
143
144            // compare maps only when window size matches
145            if (i >= window - 1 && isEqual(p_map, s_map)) {
146                res.add(i - window + 1);
147            }
148        }
149
150        return res;
151    }
152
153    private boolean isEqual(Map<String, Integer> p_map, Map<String, Integer> s_map) {
154        if (p_map.size() != s_map.size()) {
155            return false;
156        }
157        for (String k : p_map.keySet()) {
158            if (!s_map.containsKey(k) || !p_map.get(k).equals(s_map.get(k))) {
159                return false;
160            }
161        }
162        return true;
163    }

1# LC 438 Find All Anagrams in a String
2# V0
3# IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW + collections.Counter()
4class Solution(object):
5    def findAnagrams(self, s, p):
6        """
7        :type s: str
8        :type p: str
9        :rtype: List[int]
10        """
11        ls, lp = len(s), len(p)
12        cp = collections.Counter(p)
13        cs = collections.Counter()
14        ans = []
15        for i in range(ls):
16            cs[s[i]] += 1
17            if i >= lp:
18                cs[s[i - lp]] -= 1
19                ### BE AWARE OF IT
20                if cs[s[i - lp]] == 0:
21                    del cs[s[i - lp]]
22            if cs == cp:
23                ans.append(i - lp + 1)
24        return ans

3.2) Variable Size Window Examples

LC 3: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters (Template: Variable Max)

1# LC 003 Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
2# V0'
3# IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW + DICT
4#       -> use a hash table (d) record visited "element" (e.g. : a,b,c,...)
5#          (but NOT sub-string)
6class Solution(object):
7    def lengthOfLongestSubstring(self, s):
8        d = {}
9        # left pointer
10        l = 0
11        res = 0
12        # right pointer
13        for r in range(len(s)):
14            """
15            ### NOTE : we deal with "s[r] in d" case first 
16            ### NOTE : if already visited, means "repeating"
17            #      -> then we need to update left pointer (l)
18            """
19            if s[r] in d:
20                """
21                NOTE !!! this
22                -> via max(l, d[s[r]] + 1) trick,
23                   we can get the "latest" idx of duplicated s[r], and start from that one
24                """
25                l = max(l, d[s[r]] + 1)
26            # if not visited yet, record the alphabet
27            # and re-calculate the max length
28            d[s[r]] = r
29            res = max(res, r -l + 1)
30        return res
31
32# V0'
33# IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW + DICT
34#       -> use a hash table (d) record visited "element" (e.g. : a,b,c,...)
35#          (but NOT sub-string)
36class Solution(object):
37    def lengthOfLongestSubstring(self, s):
38        d = {}
39        # left pointer
40        l = 0
41        res = 0
42        # right pointer
43        for r in range(len(s)):
44            """
45            ### NOTE : we deal with "s[r] in d" case first 
46            ### NOTE : if already visited, means "repeating"
47            #      -> then we need to update left pointer (l)
48            """
49            if s[r] in d:
50                """
51                NOTE !!! this
52                -> via max(l, d[s[r]] + 1) trick,
53                   we can get the "latest" idx of duplicated s[r], and start from that one
54                """
55                l = max(l, d[s[r]] + 1)
56            # if not visited yet, record the alphabet
57            # and re-calculate the max length
58            d[s[r]] = r
59            res = max(res, r -l + 1)
60        return res

1// java
2// V0
3// IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW + HASH SET
4public int lengthOfLongestSubstring(String s) {
5
6    if (s.equals("")){
7        return 0;
8    }
9
10    if (s.equals(" ")){
11        return 1;
12    }
13
14    if (s.length() == 1){
15        return 1;
16    }
17
18    int ans = 0;
19    char[] s_array = s.toCharArray();
20    for (int i = 0; i < s_array.length-1; i++){
21        int j = i;
22        Set<String> set = new HashSet<String>();
23        while (j < s_array.length){
24            String cur = String.valueOf(s_array[j]);
25            if (set.contains(cur)){
26                ans = Math.max(ans, set.size());
27                break;
28            }else{
29                set.add(cur);
30                ans = Math.max(ans, set.size());
31                j += 1;
32            }
33        }
34    }
35
36    return ans;
37}

3.3) Counting Subarrays Examples

LC 713: Subarray Product Less Than K (Template: Counting)

1# LC 713 Subarray Product Less Than K
2# V0 
3# IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW 
4# MAINTAIN 2 INDEX : left, i, SO THE SLIDING WINDOW IS : [left, i]
5# CHECK IF THE PRODUCT OF ALL DIGITS IN THE WINDOW [left, i] < k
6# IF NOT, REMOVE CURRENT LEFT, AND DO LEFT ++
7# REPEAT ABOVE PROCESS AND GO THOROUGH ALL ARRAY  
8class Solution:
9    def numSubarrayProductLessThanK(self, nums, k):
10        # init values
11        product = 1
12        i = 0
13        result = 0
14        
15        for j, num in enumerate(nums):
16            ### NOTE : we get product first
17            product *= num
18            ### NOTE : the while loop condition : product >= k
19            #         -> if product >= k, we do the corresponding op
20            while i <= j and product >= k:
21                ### NOTE this trick
22                #    -> divided the number back, since this number already make the product > k 
23                product = product // nums[i]
24                ### NOTE : move i to 1 right index
25                i += 1
26            ### NOTE : , the number of intervals with subarray product less than k and with right-most coordinate right, is right - left + 1
27            #    -> https://leetcode.com/problems/subarray-product-less-than-k/solution/           
28            result += (j - i + 1)           
29        return result

3.4) Minimum Window Examples

LC 209: Minimum Size Subarray Sum (Template: Variable Min)

1# LC 209 Minimum Size Subarray Sum
2# V0
3# IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW : start, end
4class Solution:
5    def minSubArrayLen(self, s, nums):
6        if nums is None or len(nums) == 0:
7            return 0
8
9        n = len(nums)
10        minLength = n + 1
11        sum = 0
12        j = 0
13        for i in range(n):
14            ### NOTE the while loop condition (j < n and sum < s)
15            while j < n and sum < s:
16                sum += nums[j]
17                j += 1
18            # NOTE : we need to check if sum >= s here
19            if sum >= s:
20                minLength = min(minLength, j - i)
21
22            ### NOTE : we need to get min length of sub array
23            #          so once it meats the condition (sum >= s)
24            #          we should update the minLength (minLength = min(minLength, j - i))
25            #          and move to next i and roll back _sum (_sum -= nums[i])
26            sum -= nums[i]
27            
28        ### NOTE : if minLength == n + 1, means there is no such subarray, so return 0 instead
29        if minLength == n + 1:
30            return 0         
31        return minLength

LC 424: Longest Repeating Character Replacement (Template: Variable Max)

1# lc 424. Longest Repeating Character Replacement
2# V0
3# IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW + DICT + 2 POINTERS
4from collections import Counter
5class Solution(object):
6    def characterReplacement(self, s, k):
7        table = Counter()
8        res = 0
9        p1 = p2 = 0
10        # below can be either while or for loop
11        while p2 < len(s):
12            table[s[p2]] += 1
13            p2 += 1
14            """
15            ### NOTE : if remain elements > k, means there is no possibility to make this substring as "longest substring containing the same letter"
16               ->  remain elements = p1 - p2 - max(table.values())
17               ->  e.g. if we consider "max(table.values()" as the "repeating character", then "p2 - p1 - max(table.values()" is the count of elements we need to replace
18               ->  so we need to clear "current candidate" for next iteration
19            """
20            while p2 - p1 - max(table.values()) > k:
21                table[s[p1]] -= 1
22                p1 += 1
23            res = max(res, p2 - p1)
24        return res
25    
26# V0'
27from collections import defaultdict
28class Solution:
29    def characterReplacement(self, s, k):
30        cnt = defaultdict(int)
31        maxLen = 0
32        l = 0
33        # below can be either while or for loop
34        for r in range(len(s)):
35            cnt[s[r]] += 1
36            ### NOTE : this condition
37            while r - l + 1 - max(cnt.values()) > k:
38                cnt[s[l]] -= 1
39                l += 1
40            maxLen = max(maxLen, r - l + 1)     
41
42        return maxLen

1// java
2// LC 424
3// V2
4// IDEA : Sliding Window (Slow)
5// https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-repeating-character-replacement/editorial/
6public int characterReplacement_4(String s, int k) {
7    HashSet<Character> allLetters = new HashSet();
8
9    // collect all unique letters
10    for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
11        allLetters.add(s.charAt(i));
12    }
13
14    int maxLength = 0;
15    for (Character letter : allLetters) {
16        int start = 0;
17        int count = 0;
18        // initialize a sliding window for each unique letter
19        for (int end = 0; end < s.length(); end += 1) {
20            if (s.charAt(end) == letter) {
21                // if the letter matches, increase the count
22                count += 1;
23            }
24            // bring start forward until the window is valid again
25            while (!isWindowValid(start, end, count, k)) {
26                if (s.charAt(start) == letter) {
27                    // if the letter matches, decrease the count
28                    count -= 1;
29                }
30                start += 1;
31            }
32            // at this point the window is valid, update maxLength
33            maxLength = Math.max(maxLength, end + 1 - start);
34        }
35    }
36    return maxLength;
37}
38
39private Boolean isWindowValid(int start, int end, int count, int k) {
40    // end + 1 - start - count is different element count
41    return end + 1 - start - count <= k;
42}

3.5) Advanced Examples

LC 413: Arithmetic Slices (Template: Custom)

1# LC 413 Arithmetic Slices
2# V0
3# IDEA : SLIDING DINDOW + 2 pointers
4# STEPS:
5#   -> step 1) loop over nums from idx=2 (for i in range(2, len(A)))
6#   -> step 2) use the other pointer j, "look back to idx = 0" via while loop
7#       -> if there is any case fit condition, add to result
8#   -> step 3) return ans
9class Solution(object):
10    def numberOfArithmeticSlices(self, A):
11        # edge case
12        if not A or len(A) < 3:
13            return 0
14        res = 0
15        j = 2
16        for i in range(2, len(A)):
17            # use the other pointer j, "look back to idx = 0" via while loop
18            j = i
19            while j-2 >= 0:
20                # if there is any case fit condition, add to result
21                if A[j] - A[j-1] == A[j-1] - A[j-2]:
22                    res += 1
23                    j -= 1
24                else:
25                    break
26        return res

LC 1151: Minimum Swaps to Group All 1’s Together (Template: Fixed Size)

1# LC 1151 Minimum Swaps to Group All 1's Together
2# V0
3# IDEA : Sliding Window with Two Pointers
4# IDEA : core : Find which sub-array HAS MOST "1", since it means it needs MINIMUM SWAP for getting all "1" toogether
5# https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-swaps-to-group-all-1s-together/solution/
6class Solution:
7    def minSwaps(self, data):
8        ones = sum(data)
9        cnt_one = max_one = 0
10        left = right = 0
11        while right < len(data):
12            # updating the number of 1's by adding the new element
13            cnt_one += data[right]
14            right += 1
15            # maintain the length of the window to ones
16            if right - left > ones:
17                # updating the number of 1's by removing the oldest element
18                cnt_one -= data[left]
19                left += 1
20            # record the maximum number of 1's in the window
21            max_one = max(max_one, cnt_one)
22        return ones - max_one

LC 763: Partition Labels (Template: Custom Greedy + Sliding Window)

1// java
2// LC 763 Partition Labels
3
4// V0-2
5// IDEA: GREEDY + hashMap record last idx + sliding window (fixed by gpt)
6public List<Integer> partitionLabels_0_2(String s) {
7    List<Integer> res = new ArrayList<>();
8
9    if (s == null || s.length() == 0) {
10        return res;
11    }
12
13    // Map each character to its last index
14    Map<Character, Integer> lastIndexMap = new HashMap<>();
15    for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
16        lastIndexMap.put(s.charAt(i), i);
17    }
18
19    int l = 0;
20    while (l < s.length()) {
21        int end = lastIndexMap.get(s.charAt(l));
22        int r = l;
23
24        // Expand the window to include all characters in the current segment
25        while (r < end) {
26            end = Math.max(end, lastIndexMap.get(s.charAt(r)));
27            r++;
28        }
29
30        res.add(end - l + 1);
31        l = end + 1;
32    }
33
34    return res;
35}

LC 1838 Frequency of the Most Frequent Element

1    public int maxFrequency_0_1(int[] nums, int k) {
2        /**
3         *
4         * Sort nums in non-decreasing order.
5         * Sorting is crucial — when array is sorted,
6         * if we want to raise several elements
7         * to match some target value,
8         * the cheapest target to aim for is
9         * always the current rightmost value in a sorted window.
10         */
11        Arrays.sort(nums); // sort ascending
12
13        /**
14         * Maintain the `sum of the current sliding window. `
15         * Use long to prevent integer overflow
16         * if nums values and window size are large.
17         *
18         *
19         * -> windowSum: sum of cur slide window
20         */
21        long windowSum = 0; // use long to avoid overflow
22        // left is the left index of the sliding window. res stores the best (maximum) window size found so far.
23        // Initialize res = 1 because at least one element always fits.
24        int left = 0;
25        int res = 1;
26
27        for (int right = 0; right < nums.length; right++) {
28            windowSum += nums[right];
29
30            /**
31             *  NOTE !!! core logic:
32             *
33             *  This computes the cost to make every element in window [left..right] equal to nums[right]:
34             *
35             *   - nums[right] * windowSize is the total value
36             *     if every element became nums[right].
37             *   - Subtract windowSum (the current total) to
38             *     get how much increment is needed.
39             *
40             * If this cost exceeds k, the current window is
41             *  not achievable with at most k increments,
42             *  so we must shrink it from the left.
43             *
44             *
45             * Important: cast nums[right] to long (done by (long)) to avoid overflow in product.
46             *
47             */
48            // cost to make all numbers in window [left..right] equal to nums[right]:
49            // cost = nums[right] * windowSize - windowSum
50            // if cost > k, shrink from the left
51            /**  NOTE !!
52             *
53             *  nums[right] * (right - left + 1):  how much increment needed
54             *
55             */
56            while ((long) nums[right] * (right - left + 1) - windowSum > k) {
57                /**
58                 * Remove the leftmost element from the window sum because we are going to increment left.
59                 */
60                windowSum -= nums[left];
61                left++;
62            }
63
64            // update max length
65            res = Math.max(res, right - left + 1);
66        }
67
68        return res;
69    }

4) Core Patterns & Quick Reference

📌 Read this section after reviewing the templates (section 1) and before diving into detailed examples (sections 2-3).

4.1) Template Quick Reference

Template Time Space Key Pattern When to Use
Fixed Size O(n) O(k) for i in range(n) Window size predetermined
Variable Max O(n) O(k) for-while expand-contract Find maximum valid length
Variable Min O(n) O(k) while-while contract-expand Find minimum valid length
Counting O(n) O(k) for with count += right-left+1 Count subarrays/substrings

4.2) Common Patterns & Tricks

Character Frequency Tracking

1# Track character counts in window
2window = {}
3window[char] = window.get(char, 0) + 1
4
5# Remove character from window
6window[char] -= 1
7if window[char] == 0:
8    del window[char]

Validity Conditions

1# Common validity checks
2def is_valid_permutation(window, target):
3    return window == target
4
5def is_valid_distinct_k(window, k):
6    return len(window) <= k
7
8def is_valid_sum(current_sum, target):
9    return current_sum >= target

Result Updates

1# Maximum length problems
2max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1)
3
4# Minimum length problems  
5if is_valid:
6    min_len = min(min_len, right - left + 1)
7
8# Counting problems
9count += right - left + 1  # All subarrays ending at 'right'

4.3) Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify Pattern: Fixed size, variable max/min, or counting?
  2. Choose Template: Select appropriate template based on pattern
  3. Define Window State: HashMap, set, sum, or counter?
  4. Define Validity: What makes the window valid/invalid?
  5. Update Logic: When and how to update the result?

4.4) Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Wrong loop structure (using wrong template)
  • Forgetting to handle window state correctly
  • Incorrect validity condition logic
  • Missing edge cases (empty input, single element)

✅ Best Practices:

  • Use collections.Counter for character frequency problems
  • Always handle the case when removing elements from HashMap
  • Test with edge cases: empty string, single character, all same characters
  • Consider if the problem needs “exactly k” vs “at most k”
  • For “exactly k” problems: use “at most k - at most (k-1)”

4.5) Time & Space Complexity Analysis

  • Time: O(n) - each element visited at most twice
  • Space: O(k) where k is window size or number of unique elements
  • Optimization: Use arrays instead of HashMaps when character set is limited (e.g., only lowercase letters)

4.6) Related Algorithms

  • Two Pointers: Foundation for sliding window
  • Hash Table: For frequency tracking
  • Deque: For sliding window maximum/minimum
  • Prefix Sum: For sum-based sliding window problems

4.7) Additional Template Examples (Advanced Java Reference)

LC 1004: Max Consecutive Ones III — Variable Window (Max Length)

Expand right, shrink left when zero count exceeds k.

1// LC 1004 - Max Consecutive Ones III
2// IDEA: Sliding window — track zero count, shrink when zeroCnt > k
3// time = O(N), space = O(1)
4public int longestOnes(int[] nums, int k) {
5    int l = 0, zeroCnt = 0, ans = 0;
6    for (int r = 0; r < nums.length; r++) {
7        if (nums[r] == 0) zeroCnt++;
8        while (zeroCnt > k) {
9            if (nums[l] == 0) zeroCnt--;
10            l++;
11        }
12        ans = Math.max(ans, r - l + 1);
13    }
14    return ans;
15}

LC 3: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters — Variable Window

Shrink left pointer whenever a duplicate character enters the window.

1// LC 3 - Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
2// IDEA: Sliding window with HashSet to track characters in window
3// time = O(N), space = O(min(N, charset))
4public int lengthOfLongestSubstring(String s) {
5    Set<Character> set = new HashSet<>();
6    int l = 0, ans = 0;
7    for (int r = 0; r < s.length(); r++) {
8        while (set.contains(s.charAt(r))) {
9            set.remove(s.charAt(l++));
10        }
11        set.add(s.charAt(r));
12        ans = Math.max(ans, r - l + 1);
13    }
14    return ans;
15}

LC 76: Minimum Window Substring — Variable Window (Min Length)

Expand to include all required chars, then shrink to minimize window.

1// LC 76 - Minimum Window Substring
2// IDEA: Sliding window with frequency maps; shrink when window is valid
3// time = O(N + M), space = O(N + M)
4public String minWindow(String s, String t) {
5    Map<Character, Integer> need = new HashMap<>(), window = new HashMap<>();
6    for (char c : t.toCharArray()) need.merge(c, 1, Integer::sum);
7    int l = 0, valid = 0, start = 0, minLen = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
8    for (int r = 0; r < s.length(); r++) {
9        char c = s.charAt(r);
10        window.merge(c, 1, Integer::sum);
11        if (need.containsKey(c) && window.get(c).equals(need.get(c))) valid++;
12        while (valid == need.size()) {
13            if (r - l + 1 < minLen) { minLen = r - l + 1; start = l; }
14            char d = s.charAt(l++);
15            if (need.containsKey(d)) {
16                if (window.get(d).equals(need.get(d))) valid--;
17                window.merge(d, -1, Integer::sum);
18            }
19        }
20    }
21    return minLen == Integer.MAX_VALUE ? "" : s.substring(start, start + minLen);
22}

LC 567: Permutation in String — Fixed Window Anagram Check

Maintain character frequency of window size len(s1); check if it matches s1’s freq.

1// LC 567 - Permutation in String
2// IDEA: Fixed sliding window — track char frequencies, check match
3// time = O(N), space = O(1)
4public boolean checkInclusion(String s1, String s2) {
5    if (s1.length() > s2.length()) return false;
6    int[] need = new int[26], window = new int[26];
7    for (char c : s1.toCharArray()) need[c-'a']++;
8    int k = s1.length();
9    for (int i = 0; i < s2.length(); i++) {
10        window[s2.charAt(i)-'a']++;
11        if (i >= k) window[s2.charAt(i-k)-'a']--;
12        if (Arrays.equals(need, window)) return true;
13    }
14    return false;
15}

LC 438: Find All Anagrams in a String — Fixed Window

Same as LC 567 but collect all starting indices where anagram window matches.

1// LC 438 - Find All Anagrams in a String
2// IDEA: Fixed sliding window — collect all positions where window = anagram
3// time = O(N), space = O(1)
4public List<Integer> findAnagrams(String s, String p) {
5    List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
6    if (s.length() < p.length()) return result;
7    int[] need = new int[26], window = new int[26];
8    for (char c : p.toCharArray()) need[c-'a']++;
9    int k = p.length();
10    for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
11        window[s.charAt(i)-'a']++;
12        if (i >= k) window[s.charAt(i-k)-'a']--;
13        if (Arrays.equals(need, window)) result.add(i - k + 1);
14    }
15    return result;
16}

LC 209: Minimum Size Subarray Sum — Variable Window (Min Length)

Expand right; once sum >= target, shrink left to find minimum window length.

1// LC 209 - Minimum Size Subarray Sum
2// IDEA: Sliding window — shrink left when sum >= target, record min length
3// time = O(N), space = O(1)
4public int minSubArrayLen(int target, int[] nums) {
5    int l = 0, sum = 0, minLen = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
6    for (int r = 0; r < nums.length; r++) {
7        sum += nums[r];
8        while (sum >= target) {
9            minLen = Math.min(minLen, r - l + 1);
10            sum -= nums[l++];
11        }
12    }
13    return minLen == Integer.MAX_VALUE ? 0 : minLen;
14}

LC 424: Longest Repeating Character Replacement — Variable Window

Window is valid if (window size - max frequency) <= k; expand and track max freq.

1// LC 424 - Longest Repeating Character Replacement
2// IDEA: Sliding window — valid if windowSize - maxFreq <= k
3// time = O(N), space = O(1)
4public int characterReplacement(String s, int k) {
5    int[] freq = new int[26];
6    int l = 0, maxFreq = 0, ans = 0;
7    for (int r = 0; r < s.length(); r++) {
8        freq[s.charAt(r)-'A']++;
9        maxFreq = Math.max(maxFreq, freq[s.charAt(r)-'A']);
10        while ((r - l + 1) - maxFreq > k) freq[s.charAt(l++)-'A']--;
11        ans = Math.max(ans, r - l + 1);
12    }
13    return ans;
14}

LC 713: Subarray Product Less Than K — Sliding Window Count

Shrink left when product >= k; each valid right position contributes (r-l+1) subarrays.

1// LC 713 - Subarray Product Less Than K
2// IDEA: Sliding window — count subarrays ending at r with product < k
3// time = O(N), space = O(1)
4public int numSubarrayProductLessThanK(int[] nums, int k) {
5    if (k <= 1) return 0;
6    int l = 0, product = 1, count = 0;
7    for (int r = 0; r < nums.length; r++) {
8        product *= nums[r];
9        while (product >= k) product /= nums[l++];
10        count += r - l + 1; // all subarrays ending at r with left in [l, r]
11    }
12    return count;
13}

LC 239: Sliding Window Maximum — Monotonic Deque

Maintain a decreasing deque of indices; front is always the max of current window.

1// LC 239 - Sliding Window Maximum
2// IDEA: Monotonic decreasing deque — front = max of current window
3// time = O(N), space = O(k)
4public int[] maxSlidingWindow(int[] nums, int k) {
5    int n = nums.length;
6    int[] ans = new int[n - k + 1];
7    Deque<Integer> deque = new ArrayDeque<>(); // stores indices
8    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
9        // remove out-of-window indices
10        while (!deque.isEmpty() && deque.peekFirst() < i - k + 1) deque.pollFirst();
11        // maintain decreasing order
12        while (!deque.isEmpty() && nums[deque.peekLast()] < nums[i]) deque.pollLast();
13        deque.offerLast(i);
14        if (i >= k - 1) ans[i - k + 1] = nums[deque.peekFirst()];
15    }
16    return ans;
17}

LC 1838: Frequency of the Most Frequent Element — Sliding Window

Sort array; expand right, shrink left when cost to equalize window exceeds k.

1// LC 1838 - Frequency of the Most Frequent Element
2// IDEA: Sort + sliding window — equalize all elements in window to nums[r]
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(1)
4public int maxFrequency(int[] nums, int k) {
5    Arrays.sort(nums);
6    int l = 0, ans = 1;
7    long windowSum = 0;
8    for (int r = 1; r < nums.length; r++) {
9        windowSum += nums[r];
10        // cost to raise all window elements to nums[r]
11        while ((long) nums[r] * (r - l + 1) - windowSum > k) {
12            windowSum -= nums[l++];
13        }
14        ans = Math.max(ans, r - l + 1);
15    }
16    return ans;
17}

LC 159: Longest Substring with At Most Two Distinct Characters — Variable Window

Shrink left when distinct chars in window exceed 2; use frequency map.

1// LC 159 - Longest Substring with At Most Two Distinct Characters
2// IDEA: Sliding window with HashMap — shrink when distinct > 2
3// time = O(N), space = O(1)
4public int lengthOfLongestSubstringTwoDistinct(String s) {
5    Map<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
6    int l = 0, ans = 0;
7    for (int r = 0; r < s.length(); r++) {
8        freq.merge(s.charAt(r), 1, Integer::sum);
9        while (freq.size() > 2) {
10            char lc = s.charAt(l);
11            freq.merge(lc, -1, Integer::sum);
12            if (freq.get(lc) == 0) freq.remove(lc);
13            l++;
14        }
15        ans = Math.max(ans, r - l + 1);
16    }
17    return ans;
18}
